prompt: ASHRAE BEMP certification primarily validates competency in:

options.text: Building energy modeling software, model quality, calibration, and results interpretation
Refrigerant recovery and EPA Section 608 certification only
Structural steel connection design exclusively
Elevator traffic simulation without energy analysis

explanation: BEMP tests modeling proficiency across inputs, simulation, calibration, and reporting per the BEMP exam blueprint.

distractorExplanations.b: Refrigerant handling is a separate credential domain.
distractorExplanations.c: Structural design is outside BEMP scope.
distractorExplanations.d: BEMP focuses on whole-building energy simulation.

prompt: In a whole-building energy model, the weather file (EPW/TMY) primarily supplies:

options.text: Hourly dry-bulb temperature, humidity, solar radiation, and wind data for the simulation year
Only average monthly utility rates without climate data
Occupant names and workstation assignments
Structural load combinations for seismic design

explanation: Weather files drive HVAC loads, solar gains, and free-cooling availability in simulation engines.

distractorExplanations.b: Tariffs are separate economic inputs.
distractorExplanations.c: Occupancy is modeled via schedules, not weather files.
distractorExplanations.d: Structural loads are unrelated to energy weather files.

prompt: When calibrating an existing-building energy model to utility data, a common acceptance criterion is:

options.text: Monthly or annual simulated energy within an agreed tolerance (often ±5–15%) of billed consumption after normalization
Exact match to the penny for every fuel type every month
No comparison to utility bills is ever needed
Only peak demand matching with no energy total review

explanation: ASHRAE and industry practice use tolerance bands on normalized utility data to judge model calibration quality.

distractorExplanations.b: Perfect monthly agreement is rarely achievable or required.
distractorExplanations.c: Calibration requires measured consumption comparison.
distractorExplanations.d: Calibration typically addresses energy totals and often demand.

prompt: ASHRAE Standard 90.1 Appendix G baseline models for rating compliance are typically:

options.text: A minimally compliant reference building modeled with prescribed system types and efficiencies
Identical copies of the proposed design with no changes
Hand calculations without simulation software
Based only on architect renderings without geometry

explanation: Appendix G defines baseline assumptions so proposed design performance can be compared for code or LEED paths.

distractorExplanations.b: Baseline and proposed models differ by Appendix G rules.
distractorExplanations.c: Appendix G uses performance rating via simulation.
distractorExplanations.d: Models require defined geometry and systems.

prompt: Zoning a building energy model into thermal blocks should reflect:

options.text: Spaces with similar orientation, schedule, internal loads, and HVAC service for accurate load aggregation
Only floor level regardless of exposure or use
Random assignment to minimize file size only
One zone for the entire campus with no differentiation

explanation: Proper zoning balances accuracy and runtime while capturing load diversity and system boundaries.

distractorExplanations.b: Orientation and use strongly affect loads.
distractorExplanations.c: Zoning must represent physical and operational similarity.
distractorExplanations.d: Oversimplified zoning obscures load diversity and controls.

prompt: Internal gains from occupants in energy models are commonly represented using:

options.text: Sensible and latent heat rates per person combined with occupancy schedules
Fixed 10 kW per person regardless of activity
No internal gains because people do not affect loads
Only lighting power with no metabolic contribution

explanation: ASHRAE and software defaults assign watts/person by activity with time-varying schedules.

distractorExplanations.b: Metabolic rates vary by activity level; typical values are much lower.
distractorExplanations.c: Occupants add significant sensible and latent gains.
distractorExplanations.d: People add metabolic gains separate from plug and lighting loads.

prompt: Infiltration in detailed energy models is often specified as:

options.text: Effective leakage area, ACH, or crack flow tied to envelope airtightness and pressure difference assumptions
Always zero because modern buildings are perfectly sealed
Only through open windows with no envelope leakage
A fixed 50 ACH for all commercial buildings

explanation: Modelers use blower-door results, codes, or standards to set realistic infiltration inputs.

distractorExplanations.b: All buildings have some infiltration/exfiltration.
distractorExplanations.c: Envelope infiltration is modeled separately from intentional ventilation.
distractorExplanations.d: 50 ACH is unrealistic for typical commercial envelopes.

prompt: When comparing proposed vs baseline energy cost in a LEED energy model, the metric is typically:

options.text: Percentage improvement in cost (or energy) relative to the ASHRAE 90.1 Appendix G baseline
Absolute site EUI only with no baseline reference
Peak cooling tonnage divided by floor area only
Number of HVAC zones in the proposed model

explanation: LEED and many codes use cost or energy improvement vs a standardized baseline simulation.

distractorExplanations.b: LEED EA credits compare proposed performance to Appendix G baseline.
distractorExplanations.c: Peak load metrics do not replace cost/energy improvement calculations.
distractorExplanations.d: Zone count is not a compliance metric.

prompt: Sensitivity analysis in building energy modeling is used to:

options.text: Identify which input parameters most influence results and quantify uncertainty
Eliminate the need for any calibrated baseline
Replace all HVAC system definitions with defaults
Guarantee utility bills will match exactly next year

explanation: One-at-a-time or Monte Carlo sensitivity helps prioritize data collection and risk disclosure.

distractorExplanations.b: Sensitivity complements but does not replace calibration.
distractorExplanations.c: Sensitivity tests parameter variation; it does not remove system detail.
distractorExplanations.d: Sensitivity explores uncertainty; it cannot guarantee future bills.

prompt: Lighting power density in a model should align with:

options.text: Space-type LPD from code tables or design documents, controlled by schedules and daylighting controls where modeled
Maximum connected load of all building equipment combined
Only exterior signage loads
Structural footing sizes

explanation: Accurate LPD and control schedules affect both energy and peak demand results.

distractorExplanations.b: LPD is watts per area for lighting, not total connected load.
distractorExplanations.c: Interior and exterior lighting schedules use space-based LPD.
distractorExplanations.d: Footing design is unrelated to lighting power density.

prompt: A common reason simulated cooling energy exceeds measured data after calibration is:

options.text: Overstated internal gains, schedules, or supply air temperature assumptions not matching actual operation
Weather file always underpredicts solar gain
Models cannot simulate cooling systems
Utility meters never record cooling energy

explanation: Operational drift, control overrides, and input assumptions are frequent calibration discrepancy sources.

distractorExplanations.b: Weather files are standardized; operational assumptions often cause divergence.
distractorExplanations.c: Simulation engines model HVAC explicitly.
distractorExplanations.d: Meters capture electricity and fuels used for cooling.

prompt: On-site photovoltaic generation in an annual energy model is typically accounted by:

options.text: Reducing purchased electricity by simulated PV output using site weather and array characteristics
Adding PV kWh to heating energy use
Ignoring PV because renewables are not modellable
Counting PV only in baseline, never in proposed

explanation: Net site energy and cost metrics reflect on-site generation offsetting grid purchases.

distractorExplanations.b: PV offsets electrical consumption, not heating directly.
distractorExplanations.c: Major tools include PV production models.
distractorExplanations.d: Proposed models include on-site renewables per project scope.

prompt: The BEMP exam passing score published by ASHRAE is approximately:

options.text: 50 correct out of 100 scored items
69 correct out of 100 scored items (verify current FAQs before scheduling)
100 correct with no pretest items
83 correct out of 120 scored items

explanation: Each ASHRAE credential has its own Angoff-derived pass point; verify at ashrae.org.

distractorExplanations.a: ASHRAE BEMP pass point is higher than 50%.
distractorExplanations.c: Exams include unscored pretest items; pass is on scored items only.
distractorExplanations.d: 83/120 is the BCxP threshold, not BEMP.

prompt: Hourly schedules for plug loads in office models should reflect:

options.text: Typical weekday/weekend occupancy and equipment use patterns for each space type
Constant maximum load 24/7 all year
Zero plug load because computers use no power
Only holiday schedules with no weekday variation

explanation: Schedule shape strongly affects both annual energy and peak demand predictions.

distractorExplanations.b: Flat schedules overpredict off-hours consumption.
distractorExplanations.c: Plug loads are a major commercial building end use.
distractorExplanations.d: Weekday operation patterns dominate annual energy.

prompt: Envelope U-factor and solar heat gain coefficient inputs should come from:

options.text: Fenestration and opaque assembly product data, NFRC ratings, or code default tables when as-designed data is unavailable
Random values chosen to match utility bills after simulation
Only carpet color specifications
Structural steel yield strength

explanation: Defensible envelope inputs come from specifications, testing, or recognized default libraries.

distractorExplanations.b: Inputs should be evidence-based; post-hoc tuning without basis is poor practice.
distractorExplanations.c: Carpet color does not define thermal transmittance.
distractorExplanations.d: Structural properties differ from thermal envelope properties.

prompt: When modeling a VAV system with reheat, a critical input is:

options.text: Minimum airflow setpoints and reheat coil operation at part load to capture simultaneous heating/cooling penalties
Only supply fan horsepower with no zone controls
Chiller plant cooling tower color
Excluding all zone-level controls from the model

explanation: VAV reheat models must represent minimum ventilation and terminal reheat to avoid unrealistic savings.

distractorExplanations.b: Zone minimums and reheat drive significant reheat energy.
distractorExplanations.c: Cosmetic attributes do not affect energy simulation.
distractorExplanations.d: VAV behavior requires zone airflow and reheat logic.

prompt: Unmet load hours reported by simulation indicate:

options.text: Hours when zone temperatures could not be maintained within the thermostat deadband under modeled capacity
Hours when the utility company shut off power
Total annual electricity consumption
Number of occupants in the building

explanation: Excessive unmet hours signal undersized equipment or control issues in the model.

distractorExplanations.b: Unmet hours are a simulation comfort/capacity metric.
distractorExplanations.c: Unmet hours count comfort violations, not kWh.
distractorExplanations.d: Occupancy is a separate schedule input.

prompt: ASHRAE Guideline 14 is often referenced in BEMP practice for:

options.text: Measurement of energy and demand savings and model calibration uncertainty reporting
Kitchen exhaust hood grease filtration only
Elevator inspection checklists
Refrigerant purity testing procedures

explanation: Guideline 14 provides statistical criteria for calibrated models and savings measurements.

distractorExplanations.b: Guideline 14 addresses M&V and calibration statistics.
distractorExplanations.c: Elevator codes are unrelated to Guideline 14.
distractorExplanations.d: Refrigerant testing follows EPA and industry standards, not Guideline 14.

prompt: Selecting an appropriate baseline HVAC system type under Appendix G requires:

options.text: Following prescriptive system type rules based on building type, climate zone, and fuel availability
Copying the proposed system's brand name into baseline
Always using the highest efficiency chiller available commercially
Omitting HVAC systems from the baseline entirely

explanation: Appendix G system mapping tables standardize baseline assumptions by building and climate.

distractorExplanations.b: Baseline equipment types are prescribed, not copied from design.
distractorExplanations.c: Baseline efficiencies are defined by standard tables, not best available.
distractorExplanations.d: Baseline models include fully defined HVAC systems.

prompt: A quality control review of an energy model should verify:

options.text: Geometry sanity, schedule plausibility, input units, version control, and alignment with design documents
Only the color theme of simulation reports
That no weather file was used
Deletion of all internal gains to simplify results

explanation: Peer review catches unit errors, orientation mistakes, and schedule outliers before results are published.

distractorExplanations.b: QC focuses on technical accuracy and traceability.
distractorExplanations.c: Weather files are required for dynamic simulation.
distractorExplanations.d: Removing internal gains would distort load predictions.

prompt: Bin analysis of hourly simulation output helps modelers:

options.text: Relate energy use to temperature bins to explain weather sensitivity and validate trends
Design structural foundations for high-rise buildings
Calculate refrigerant charge without measurements
Replace all HVAC sizing calculations

explanation: Plotting kWh vs outdoor temperature reveals heating/cooling balance and calibration alignment.

distractorExplanations.b: Bin analysis is an energy post-processing technique.
distractorExplanations.c: Refrigerant charge requires field protocols, not bin analysis.
distractorExplanations.d: Bin analysis supports interpretation; it does not replace sizing.

prompt: When documenting model assumptions for a client report, BEMP best practice includes:

options.text: A clear table of key inputs, data sources, software version, and known limitations
Hiding all assumptions so results cannot be questioned
Reporting only a single annual kWh number with no context
Deleting prior model versions from the project record

explanation: Documented assumptions enable third-party review and future updates.

distractorExplanations.b: Transparency is required for defensible consulting deliverables.
distractorExplanations.c: Clients need traceability to inputs and methods.
distractorExplanations.d: Version history supports audit trails.

prompt: Modeling district energy or campus steam supply typically requires:

options.text: Defining purchased utility rates, plant efficiency or allocation method, and boundary conditions for site vs source energy
Assuming free infinite steam with no metering
Ignoring all thermal losses in distribution
Modeling only electrical loads with no thermal plants

explanation: Site/source energy reporting depends on how purchased district energy is characterized.

distractorExplanations.b: District systems have measured or allocated energy costs.
distractorExplanations.c: Distribution losses affect site and source energy accounting.
distractorExplanations.d: District steam/chilled water must be represented at boundaries.

prompt: Pretest items on the ASHRAE BEMP exam are:

options.text: Always the first 15 questions marked in the candidate guide
Optional bonus questions that raise the score above 100%
Unidentified questions that do not count toward the passing score
Only field-experience essays

explanation: Most ASHRAE exams embed unscored pretest items throughout the form.

distractorExplanations.a: Pretest items are not identified to candidates during the exam.
distractorExplanations.b: Pretest items are unscored and not bonus points.
distractorExplanations.d: BEMP uses multiple-choice items, including unscored pretest questions.

prompt: Comparing multiple ECM scenarios in a single model file is best done by:

options.text: Using parametric runs or separate cases with consistent weather, schedules, and calibration baseline
Changing weather files between ECMs to maximize savings appearance
Adjusting occupancy to zero for all ECMs except the preferred one
Running each ECM in a different software without documenting differences

explanation: Isolated ECM runs against a calibrated base case produce defensible savings estimates.

distractorExplanations.b: ECM comparisons require consistent weather and base assumptions.
distractorExplanations.c: Inconsistent schedules invalidate comparisons.
distractorExplanations.d: Comparisons should be methodologically consistent and documented.
